title: Applying oil finish to bare wood furniture
subtitle: Feed bare or stripped wood with danish oil or tung oil in multiple thin coats to a satin finish
excerpt: How to finish bare or stripped wooden furniture with a penetrating oil. Covers surface preparation, the correct application technique for thin coats, the critical between-coat sanding step, and the curing period before use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of polymerised linseed or tung oil with drying agents, thinners, and sometimes a resin. Penetrates into the wood surface and cures in place, enhancing the grain and providing a water-resistant finish. Does not form a thick surface film the way varnish or lacquer does, so it is re-coatable without stripping.

Rags soaked in drying oils (danish oil, tung oil, boiled linseed oil) can self-ignite through an exothermic oxidation reaction, sometimes hours after use. Lay used rags flat in a single layer outdoors until dry, then dispose in a metal bin. Never bunch or fold them in a pile.

content.text: An oil finish is the most forgiving wood finish to apply. Thin coats are easy to achieve, mistakes sand back easily, and the result shows the wood grain clearly. One safety step cannot be skipped:
, drying oils generate heat as they harden and will self-ignite if the rags are left in a pile.

Sand the bare wood through the grits: 120, then 180, finishing with 240. Sand along the grain, cross-grain scratches show through an oil finish. After the final grit, wipe the surface with a tack cloth or a barely damp rag to remove dust. Allow to dry for 10 minutes. Any old finish residue must be removed before oiling,
cannot penetrate through wax or lacquer.
Step 2, Apply the first coat liberally

Brush a generous first coat onto the wood and allow it to soak in for 20 to 30 minutes. Keep the surface wet, add more oil wherever it looks dry and dull. After 30 minutes, wipe off all excess oil with a clean dry rag. The surface should look slightly shiny but have no pooled oil sitting on top. Leave to cure for the time stated on the tin (typically 8 to 12 hours in a warm room).
Step 3, Sand between coats and apply second coat

After curing, lightly sand the surface with 320-grit paper on the sanding block. This removes the raised grain and any dust nibs. The surface will look scuffed, this is normal and correct. Wipe off the dust, then apply the second coat in the same way as the first. Wipe off excess, leave to dry again. A third coat applied in the same way gives the finished depth. Do not leave wet oil on the surface overnight, tacky oil picks up dust and never fully hardens.

After each session, lay every rag that contacted oil flat in a single layer on a concrete or tarmac surface outdoors. Do not fold, bunch, or bag them. Leave until dry (usually overnight), then dispose in a metal bin. This step is not optional, drying-oil rags have started house fires.
